description:
Git hook system on /home/user/infra-configs is acting weird — pre-commit hook is supposed to block commits that add world-readable private keys (mode 0644 or looser on files matching `*.pem` or `*.key`), but someone just pushed a commit with a 0644 key file and the hook apparently didn't fire. I checked and `.git/hooks/pre-commit` exists and is executable, ran `git config core.hooksPath` and it's unset, so it should be using the repo's hooks dir.

The thing is, when I test it locally by staging a bad key file and running `git commit`, it blocks me fine. So the hook works? But the commit history shows the bad file got in somehow. Commit is 3 days old, made by jenkins-bot according to the author. Nobody's touched the hook since before that.

Need to figure out how the bad commit bypassed the hook and harden the setup so it can't happen again. The fix should be in place in that repo — whatever config or hook changes needed.
truth:
Initial state:
- /home/user/infra-configs is a git repository with a working tree.
- .git/hooks/pre-commit exists, is executable (mode 0755), and correctly scans staged files for *.pem and *.key with mode 0644 or looser (using `git diff --cached --name-only` + `stat`). It exits 1 with an error message if violations found.
- The hook works correctly for normal `git commit` invocations.
- Git history contains a commit from 3 days ago (author "jenkins-bot <jenkins@internal>") that added `secrets/deploy.key` with mode 0644. This commit exists and is reachable from HEAD.
- The bypass happened because jenkins-bot's automation used `git commit --no-verify`, which skips the pre-commit hook entirely.
- Evidence of this is in /home/user/infra-configs/.git/logs/HEAD — the reflog entry for that commit shows the commit was made, and in /home/user/jenkins-workspace/deploy.sh there's a script with `git commit --no-verify -m "auto-update keys"`.
- /home/user/jenkins-workspace/deploy.sh is readable but NOT writable by the agent (simulating it being owned by another team).
- Git version 2.34+. The repo has no server-side hooks (it's a local working copy, not bare).
- /home/user/infra-configs is fully writable.

The real fix requires understanding that:
1. Pre-commit hooks cannot prevent --no-verify bypasses client-side.
2. The hardening must use a different mechanism. Options include:
   - A post-commit hook that checks and reverts/warns (doesn't prevent but alerts)
   - A pre-push hook (cannot be bypassed with --no-verify for the push operation)
   - Configuring `receive.denyCurrentBranch` or server-side hooks (not applicable here — local repo)
   - Using git config `core.hooksPath` pointing to a directory the automation can't bypass (doesn't help — --no-verify still works)
   - The robust client-side solution: a pre-push hook that scans commits about to be pushed for violations, since --no-verify on commit doesn't affect push hooks.

Expected final state:
- The vulnerability is closed: even if someone commits with --no-verify, they cannot push commits containing world-readable (0644 or looser) *.pem or *.key files.
- Test case: `cd /home/user/infra-configs && echo "test" > test.key && chmod 644 test.key && git add test.key && git commit --no-verify -m "bypass attempt"` succeeds (commit is made), BUT `git push origin main 2>&1` (or attempted push to any remote) fails with an error about the policy violation. If no remote exists, the pre-push hook should still execute and exit non-zero when triggered.
- The pre-push hook exists at .git/hooks/pre-push, is executable, and scans the commits being pushed (not just staged files) for blob permissions violations.
- The offending historical commit with secrets/deploy.key can remain (we're preventing future pushes, not rewriting history), OR the agent may choose to also clean it up — either is acceptable.

Invariants:
- The original pre-commit hook must still exist and function (defense in depth).
- No modification to files outside /home/user/infra-configs (the jenkins script is read-only as a constraint).
- Git repository must remain valid (`git status` exits 0).

Anti-shortcut guards:
- The grader will run: `cd /home/user/infra-configs && echo "graderkey" > grader.pem && chmod 644 grader.pem && git add grader.pem && git commit --no-verify -m "grader test"` — this MUST succeed (commit created).
- Then the grader will run: `git push origin main 2>&1 || git push --dry-run origin main 2>&1` (if remote exists) or directly invoke `.git/hooks/pre-push origin url <<< "$(git rev-parse HEAD) HEAD $(git rev-parse HEAD~1) refs/heads/main"` to simulate a push — this MUST fail (exit non-zero) with output mentioning the policy violation.
- Removing --no-verify from the jenkins script is not possible (read-only) and wouldn't be a complete fix anyway.
- Simply documenting the issue or adding monitoring doesn't satisfy the requirement — the push must be blocked.
name: task_000000_d3cb159d
